data.title: Youdao: Tepid Revenue Growth Will Ultimately Limit Earnings Growth
data.description:
Youdao (NYSE:DAO), a developer and supplier of technologies needed to provide online learning content, applications and solutions, released its most recent earnings report on August 14, which shows how DAO continues to make significant progress in a key area that is in urgent need of improvement. However, DAO needs to show more because if current trends hold, particularly as it relates to revenue growth and gross margin, long DAO is unlikely to be a winning play. Why will be covered next.

DAO has gone sideways

A previous article from last March mentioned how DAO had followed a trendline on the way higher, a rally that was assisted by enthusiasm surrounding artificial intelligence or AI, but this rally came to an end after the stock fell below the trendline, in large part because of an FY2025 outlook that called for only modest growth, which seemed contrary to the promise of AI as an enabler of faster growth.

The breakdown in the stock suggested more downside ahead as DAO searched for support to halt the decline, which the article speculated could be at one of the Fibonacci retracement levels. This was deemed to be most likely the 61.8% retracement level, or $6.28, a price level well below where DAO was at the time. Accordingly, I decided to rate DAO a hold in the article.

The chart above showed how the article hit the nail on the head on in terms of picking the likely end point for the decline in the stock because the stock proceeded to hit a low of $6.30 on April 9, before reversing course. This is just $0.02 above $6.28, or where the previous article suggested the stock might be able to find support and stem the decline in the stock.

Recall how the stock hit a 52-weeks high of $11.82 in February 2025, which was the culmination of a rally that started with a low of $2.86 in August 2024. The 61.8% Fibonacci retracement of $2.86 to $11.82 is $6.28, which suggests DAO has found the support needed to not only put an end to the decline in the stock, but also from where it can move higher.

However, while there was a substantial bounce following the April low, DAO has moved mostly sideways in recent weeks. Nothing thus far has been able to alter the inability to move ahead, which includes the release of the most recent or Q2 FY2025 report. The report was followed by a spike in the stock, but it surrendered most of the post-earnings gains almost immediately. DAO looks to be stuck in the $8-10 region for now.

DAO is making progress according to the latest updates

The stock has not done all that much in recent weeks, but DAO is still worth about three times as much compared to a year ago with a market cap of $1,086M. This would not have happened without the rally in the stock, which in turn would not have been possible without the current investor enthusiasm for AI and ways to play the boom in AI. DAO has been focusing on the application of AI, which turned DAO into the type of company people are very interested in as an AI play.

On the other hand, DAO also has what could be seen by many as a red flag in the sense that it has more liabilities than assets, which means DAO is insolvent on a balance sheet basis. This is not something that cannot be fixed, nor does it have to lead to something shareholders will come to regret, but it does make DAO a risky investment since it is under a certain degree of financial stress.

This is why any gain in the income statement is important, because it provides a pathway for DAO to get out of the undesirable position the balance sheet finds itself in, something AI can assist with. As mentioned earlier, the rally from earlier in the year got derailed after FY2025 guidance came in underwhelming in terms of the growth outlook, but DAO has still been making significant improvements to the income statement, including in the latest report.

In the Q2 FY2025 report, DAO reported a non-GAAP profit of RMB0.10 or $0.01 per ADS on revenue of RMB1,417.5M or $197.9M. In comparison, DAO reported a non-GAAP loss of RMB0.82 per ADS on revenue of RMB1,321.7M in Q2 FY2024. In terms of GAAP, DAO reported a loss of $0.02 per ADS, but this was also much better than the loss of $0.12 per ADS a year ago.

DAO was able to greatly improve the bottom line even though the top line grew modestly, mostly because of a decrease in operating expenses like sales and marketing, and research and development to a lesser extent. DAO finished the quarter with total assets of RMB1,841.1M or $257.0M, but this was more than offset by total liabilities of RMB3,851.4M or $537.6M on the balance sheet, which is a slight improvement compared to last year.

Is DAO improving fast enough?

DAO earned $0.10 in terms of GAAP and 0.11 in terms of non-GAAP per ADS in FY2024 and it has earned another $0.07 and $0.11, respectively, in H1 FY2025. Assuming DAO earns another $0.11 in the second half of the year, then my estimate for FY2025 non-GAAP earnings is to end up at $0.22 per ADS. The stock closed at $9.08 per ADS on August 22, which implies DAO is trading at 41.3x FY2025 earnings, using the FY2025 estimate of $0.22 per ADS.

According to the methodology favored by Peter Lynch, DAO therefore needs to grow EPADS at a CAGR of 41.3% in the next 3-5 years to be considered fairly valued at current valuations relative to growth. In order to hit this rate of growth, FY2028 EPADS would need to hit $0.63. On the surface, adding another $0.41 to FY2025 EPADS of $0.22 in three years looks like something that is feasible. DAO after all is on track to more than double earnings this year and if it can maintain this pace in the next several years, then $0.63 at the end of FY2028 is not only achievable, but something that can be surpassed.

However, there is reason to believe that the above may not pan out. This is because a major deceleration in the rate of earnings improvement in the coming years is not out of the question. Most of the earnings improvement in the latest quarter, for instance, was due to a decrease in operating expenses, but this is not sustainable in the long run.

Operating expenses decreased by RMB128.7M YoY in Q2 FY2025. In comparison non-GAAP net income attributable to shareholders flipped from a loss of RMB95.97M in Q2 FY2024 to a profit of RMB12.52M in Q2 FY2025, or almost as much as what operating expenses fell by. In other words, if not for the decrease in expenses on sales, marketing, research and development, the bottom line would have seen no improvement.

The issue here is that while there is growth in the top line, it is not growing fast enough to enable sufficient growth in the bottom line, which is why DAO needs to resort to other areas to make up for it, including by spending less on certain operating expenses. It does not help that gross margins have steadily decreased for the last two years.

However, while this approach can improve earnings in the short term, it is unlikely to be a viable method over the long run. If earnings growth is to be sustainable, at a level that corresponds to valuations, faster revenue growth is needed, more than what DAO has shown recently. Revenue growth has actually gradually slowed down in recent years.

Revenue only grew by 2% in the last completed fiscal or FY2024 after growing by 4% the year before. In the first half of the current fiscal, revenue was almost flat with RMB2,715.8M in H1 FY2025, a little better than the RMB2,713.6M in H1 FY2024. It is difficult to see how earnings can keep improving if this pattern holds. If the income statement stalls and it does not improve, DAO will be hard-pressed to eliminate the $280.6M gap between liabilities and assets in order to get rid of the label of being insolvent.

There is therefore reason to doubt the target of a CAGR of 41.3% for earnings growth will be achieved by DAO. If growth comes in significantly below, which will happen if revenue growth retains its current pace, then DAO could be overvalued relative to expected growth and therefore not worth buying at current levels.

Investor takeaways

DAO has done quite well in the last 12 months. Market cap, for instance, has tripled thanks to a huge rally in the stock. The stock did experience a correction after FY2025 guidance was not as strong as some hoped for, but DAO was able to reverse the decline to retain most gains after finding support at a key level.

The income statement has shown major gains, including in the latest report. The Q2 FY2025 report, for example, included income from operation that was not in the red for the first time in a second quarter. The Q2 FY2025 report showed that YTD non-GAAP EPADS has already equaled the $0.11 earned in all of last year and that is with one half to go in the current fiscal.

The public’s response to various AI-enabled products from DAO seems to be positive. Youdao Lingshi has gotten a good response, and so too has the Youdao Pen, which managed to hit top rankings in term of sales on e-commerce sites. Online ad services is also making good progress, including by incorporating AI.

However, the stock has stalled more recently. This is probably warranted after the huge run-up in price it had, but there is also reason to question whether DAO has what it takes to move the stock higher in the long run. There is reason to doubt whether DAO will be able to keep improving the income statement, which is needed to fix the balance sheet.

While DAO has improved earnings, there are a number of worrying signs that point to a deceleration in growth ahead to the point that it will come in at a rate that is below that needed to justify current valuations. Revenue is growing, but too slowly and gross margins keep ticking down. DAO has managed to offset this with cutting costs, but this cannot substitute for a lack of revenue growth in the long run. At this time, if current trends holds, DOA looks to be heading for a major slowdown in earnings growth.

DAO has more liabilities than it has assets and to get out of this situation DAO needs to improve the income statement, which used to come with pretty hefty losses. DAO has made significant progress in this regard, but its ability to keep making progress is in doubt as long as revenue and margins are going in the opposite direction of where they need to go. It is not likely to happen in the short term, but it will happen eventually as things stand.

I am thus neutral on DAO with everything above in mind. If long DAO is to pay off in the long run, it needs to accompany the current improvement in earnings with faster revenue growth to be sustainable, in addition to a reversal in the decline in margins, or at least a stabilization. As long as this has yet to happen, DAO is not going to become solvent.

data.title: PayPal: Weak TPV Trends Push Growth Recovery Out To 2026
data.description:
No Inflection in the Near Term

PayPal's (NASDAQ:PYPL)'s stock has been under pressure, down nearly 20% YTD despite the overall market rally. This signals that the company's fundamentals have deteriorated. At first glance, we saw PYPL's volume per transaction and customer accounts showed improvements, but Total Payment Volume (TPV) growth has failed to turn around. This can largely impact its top-line growth. The 2Q TPV was largely affected by weaker Branded checkout, driven by tariffs impact. However, it's encouraging to see margin recovery in 2Q, but the 3Q outlook suggests a margin contraction. I believe growth reacceleration remains a key catalyst to push the stock higher, but the timing is still uncertain.

In my last 3Q FY2024 analysis, I downgraded PYPL from Buy to Hold, expecting a growth slowdown ahead. Since then, the stock has dropped 20%, compared to an 8% gain for the S&P 500. We saw a major growth slowdown in 1Q FY2025, with revenue growing only 1.2% YoY, down from 9.4% YoY in 1Q FY2024. Despite PYPL beating both revenue and non-GAAP EPS in 2Q FY2025, I still don't see growth inflection until CY2026 (assuming no major economic slowdown or recession), given weak pricing power from merchant pressure and slow adoption of new checkout products. That said, I maintain a Hold rating on PYPL, though it's trading at a low valuation.

Total Payment Volume Remains Weak

PYPL's transaction revenue (90% of total revenue) is calculated as TPV multiplied by its transaction take rate. Since take rates normally come down due to industry competition, we need to see a strong transaction revenue growth, supported by a growth reacceleration in TPV.

Let's look at this chart. After showing a modest pickup in FY2023, TPV has continued to slow. In 2Q FY2025, TPV grew 6.4% YoY (only 5% on a constant currency), which translated into 4% YoY transaction revenue growth, down from 9.1% YoY a year ago.

During the 2Q FY2025 earnings call, management noted that weak TPV growth was largely driven by a flat 5% growth in Branded checkout (high margin), weighed down by Asia tariffs. In addition, Payment Service Provider (PSP) which includes low margin unbranded checkout, grew only 2% YoY, in-line with 1Q FY2025. However, they expect Braintree (part of PSP)'s TPV to reach a major inflection in 2H FY2025. This is a big deal as PSP counts for a large part of TPV.

On the positive side, Venmo remains a bright spot, with TPV growth accelerating to 12%, the highest in 3 years. Buy Now, Pay Later (BNPL) also maintained strong growth momentum with over 20% TPV growth. Despite all these achievements, these segments remain a small part of PYPL's overall revenue

FY2025 Guidance Still Suggests Growth Slowdown

Looking forward, PYPL's 3Q FY2025 outlook does not appear to be exciting. Transaction margin dollars are expected to grow at 4% YoY at the midpoint, showing sequential slowdown from 6.5% YoY in 2Q, and from 8.5% YoY a year ago. FY2025 transaction margin dollars are expected to grow 4.5% YoY at the midpoint, which also indicates a deceleration from 7% YoY in FY2024.

On the earnings call, management guided to 3Q FY2025 revenue growth "at the lower end of mid-single digits or approximately 4%". This suggests that weak growth momentum continues, driven by muted TPV growth. While the company did raise its FY2025 guidance, I believe 2025 is a transition year for PYPL, with potential reacceleration more likely in 2026 as checkout monetization scales globally and PSP TPV rebounds. However, given the ongoing economic slowdown, PYPL's recovery could be delayed further if consumer spending weakens meaningfully.

A Big Jump in Operating Cost in 3Q FY2025

Margins are expanding on a year-over-year basis. In 2Q, non-GAAP operating margin was 19.8%, up from 18.5% a year ago. However, management guided to "high single-digit non-transaction operating expense growth" in 3Q FY2025, showing a significant increase compared to the low single digit trend earlier. This will likely pressure 3Q operating margin and lower EPS growth.

As 3Q guidance shows, the midpoint of EPS suggests flat YoY growth. Although full year 2025 EPS guidance points to 6%-10% growth, this implies a slowdown in 2H FY2025, given nearly 20% YoY growth in 1H.

Falling Back into a Value Trap?

As shown in the chart, PYPL's P/E TTM has fallen below Visa (V) and Mastercard (MA) since early 2023, and the gap continues to widen. While V and MA are not direct competitors, we can see that the market gives them much higher valuations, because they could deliver growth accelerations. By contrast, PYPL's revenue continues to decelerate.

In payments and checkout, PYPL's more direct competitors are Apple Pay, Google Pay, Stripe, and Block (XYZ). Some of them have gained more consumer attention in recent years, limiting PYPL's market share expansion. Particularly, Block's Cash App is a major competitor to Venmo, and has been gaining more user growth.

That said, PYPL remains a show-me story given its near-term growth challenge, which justifies a lower valuation. As a result, I maintain a neutral view on the stock, as the risk of a potential value trap could persist if the company continues to delay its growth inflection.

The Bottom Line

While PYPL's valuation is low, trading at 13x non-GAAP P/E fwd, the continued growth slowdown in TPV and transaction revenue suggests that market share loss and yet no near-term growth inflection from next-gen products.

Looking back to 2021, PYPL traded at +50x P/E, as investors priced in a premium valuation with sustained high growth. This could significantly bring down the multiple. However, with growth sharply slowing in recent years, the stock repriced lower, not because of revenue or earnings declines, but because investors no longer expect that premium growth profile.

PYPL's Branded checkout and PSP still looked weak in 2Q FY2025 and 3Q guidance implying flat non-GAAP EPS, I believe the growth story remains on hold. Even though a potential rebound could come in 2H FY2025, it's still too early to tell. Therefore, I maintain a Hold on PYPL due to the lack of near-term catalysts, though the stock's long-term downside risk seems limited.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Contradictory Message Of Chair Powell's Jackson Hole Address
data.description:
Market Interprets Powell's Remarks As Dovish

There was much anticipation in the financial markets this past week regarding Fed Chair Powell's opening remarks at the Kansas City Fed's Jackson Hole Economic Symposium.

I turned on his speech at 10:00 AM Friday, and immediately saw the markets had a very positive reaction. Before Chair Powell even uttered a word, the Dow Jones Industrial Average was up 700 points and the yield on the 2-year Treasury Note yield had fallen by 10 basis points. This was based on the Fed's simultaneous on-line release of Chair Powell's remarks.

By the time he started speaking shortly after 10:00 AM, the Bloomberg Channel had already posted a headline at the bottom of the screen "Fed to cut rates in September" and the markets just continued to climb.

The key phrase came about one third of the way through Chair Powell's talk when he said "with policy in restrictive territory, the baseline outlook and the shifting balance of risks may warrant adjusting our policy stance."

This was no where near as definitive a declaration as last year's "A time has come for policy to adjust", but the market liked it more. Last year the Dow jumped 462 points, or 1.1% on Powell's comments, while this year the gain for the day was 846 points, or 1.9%.

The Dow surged to a record intraday high and closed at 45,632, a 10.8% gain y/y.

Powell's View Of Economic Conditions And Economic Outlook

Chair Powell highlighted how this year the economy faces many new challenges, from significantly higher tariffs, to a tighter immigration policy, and to fiscal policy changes in taxes and spending. Together these changes will have important implications for economic growth, although there is significant uncertainty about impact they will have on the economy.

On the labor front, Chair Powell addressed the large downward revisions to payroll job growth in July, which now show an average of only 35,000 new jobs for the past three months, which is down from the 168,000 new jobs per month for all of 2024. The unemployment rate, which edged up to 4.2%, still stands at a historically low rate and has been in a narrow range for the past year.

Overall, Powell felt that the labor market was in balance, but it is because of the unusual situation of slower job growth offset by fewer workers due to the new immigration policies, which suggests that the downside risks to employment are rising. He said "if those risks materialize, they can do so quickly in the form of sharply higher layoffs and rising unemployment."

As for inflation, Chair Powell acknowledged that higher tariffs are beginning to push up prices. He estimated that the soon to be released Core PCE, the Fed's preferred method of measuring inflation, ticked up to 2.9%, higher than it was a year ago.

The impact of tariffs on consumer prices is now "clearly visible," and the Fed expects that tariffs will continue to "accumulate over coming months, with high uncertainty about timing and amounts."

The key question for the Fed, though, "is whether these price increases are likely to materially raise the risk of an ongoing inflation problem. A reasonable base case is that the effects will be relatively short lived - a one-time shift in the price level."

He felt that the impact of tariffs will not all be felt at once, and will work their way through supply chains. Additionally, tariff rates might continue to evolve.

In my view, Chair Powell is suggesting that the base case is that tariffs will be a one-time price increase, and therefore the Fed will look through them, while the downside risks to labor are more significant and will be addressed with a policy adjustment, most likely a 25 basis point cut.

The Fed's Contradictory Message

Chair Powell spent two-thirds of his time speaking about the Fed's new monetary policy framework, which addresses how the Fed will operate to achieve their mandate from Congress to foster maximum employment and stable prices.

This framework review was last conducted in 2020, which was a completely different economic environment, when the Fed had to deal with interest rates being at their effective lower bound (ELB), following the Global Financial Crisis of 2008.

The ELB limited the Fed's ability to use cutting rates as a monetary policy tool, because they already were effectively at 0.0%, and therefore required a different set of tools. Today, with interest rates substantially higher, the ELB is not a primary focus.

The new framework contains two key elements.

The first is that the Fed continues to view 2.0% as their long-run inflation target that is consistent with their mandate of stable prices.

The second is how the Fed will approach periods when their inflation and employment objectives are not complementary. They will "take into account the extent of departures from our goals and the potentially different time horizons over which each is projected to return to a level consistent with our dual mandate."

These two key elements are relevant to today, because as Chair Powell stated, "in the near term, risks to inflation [are] being tilted to the upside and risks to employment to the downside-a challenging situation. When our goals are in tension like this, our framework calls for us to balance both sides of our dual mandate."

Here's the contradiction:

When Chair Powell suggested that the Fed was concerned about the downside risks to employment rising, but they will look through the one-time price increase from tariff inflation, and therefore may adjust policy by cutting rates, he is completely ignoring the new framework he just outlined for how to deal with conflicts in dual mandates.

The framework will look at the "the extent of departure from our goals and the potentially different time horizons over which each is projected to return a level consistent with our dual mandate."

To follow this framework through, Chair Powell just stated in the first paragraph of his address that "the labor market remains near maximum employment, and inflation (is) still somewhat elevated."

So, the starting point is that employment is near maximum employment and inflation is still elevated.

The Core PCE has been above the 2.0% target for four years, and before the impact of the tariffs kick in, which will push it further away from its target, it is already, at 2.8%, 40% above target.

The downside risks to employment, which the Fed is only concerned may lead to rising layoffs and unemployment, have not yet materialized. As Powell stated "other indicators of labor market conditions are also little changed or have softened only modestly, including quits, layoffs, the ratio of vacancies to unemployment and nominal wage growth."

So, if the Fed were to follow the new framework by looking at how far each of the dual mandates is from its goal and how long it will take to get back to target, the primary concern would be inflation and not the labor market. In fact, the minutes from the July FOMC meeting stated that "A majority of participants judged the upside risk to inflation as the greater of these two risks." Based on the new framework, the Fed should be considering raising rates.

Yet the market's interpretation of Powell's comments is that the Fed will cut rates in September.

Is The Political Pressure Getting To The Fed?

President Trump has made no secret of his desire for lower rates and has been extremely critical of Chair Powell. The frequency and intensity of his attacks have only increased over the past seven months.

Initially President Trump threatened to fire Chair Powell, but a Supreme Court ruling in May specifically cited the Fed and Chair Powell as being protected from President Trump's axe.

But that didn't stop President Trump from trying to force his will over the Federal Reserve.

Seeking Alpha Contributing Analyst Damir Tokic wrote an excellent piece "The Fed Takeover: It's Mission Impossible" outlining the Fed's structure, and how unlikely it would be for President Trump to be able to influence Fed policy. He indicated that the President would need at least four Fed Governors on his side. Recent events, however, have changed the probability.

While President Trump cannot remove Fed Governors for policy disputes, there is a loophole which allows the President to fire someone for cause.

With regard to Chair Powell, the administration is focused on Powell's mismanagement of the renovation of the Fed's headquarters. When asked by reporters if this is a fireable offense, President Trump replied "I sort of think it is." For a full description of the lengths the administration is going to in order to investigate the cost overruns, please see my Seeking Alpha Article "Governor Waller Gets Candid About Fed's Balance Sheet Mismatch and Losses" here.

Just this past week Fed Governor Lisa Cook, whose term expires in 2038, was accused of mortgage fraud by Bill Pulte, the director of the Federal Housing Finance Agency. Without any due process, President Trump, on Truth Social, immediately demanded her resignation. He said he would fire her if she didn't resign. This is ironic, in that Trump himself was found liable for civil fraud in a New York Court for inflating the value of his real estate properties. (While the financial penalties were overturned on appeal for being excessive, the liability for civil fraud was upheld.)

Governor Cook has responded, "I have no intention of being bullied to step down from my position because of some questions raised in a tweet."

On August 1, Fed Governor Adriana Kugler, whose term was set to expire on January 31, 2026, unexpectedly stepped down effective August 8th. President Trump immediately nominated Stephen Miran, his Chairman of the Council of Economic Advisers, to replace her.

Nick Timiraos, who covers the Fed for the Wall Street Journal, "The attacks all fit within a broader effort by Trump and his allies to make life so uncomfortable for Fed officials - first Powell, and now Cook - that they cave or quit, clearing the way to get the lower rates Trump craves or to install more loyalists who will deliver."

Conclusion

As of Friday's close, the CME FedWatch suggested an 85% probability that the Fed would cut rates by 25 basis points at the September FOMC meeting. That is up from only 60% one month ago.

The market has been clamoring for a rate cut all year, and with Chair Powell's Jackson Hole Speech, it looks like they will finally get it.

Both the stock and bond markets have responded favorably to the news.

There are still a few more data points that the Fed will get before their September 17th vote that could impact their final decision, including the September 5th unemployment report for August, as well as PPI and CPI inflation numbers the following week.

These market expectations, however, don't address the Fed's contradiction between the way Chair Powell outlined his views that the balance of risks are more weighted to potential downsides in employment, which presently is near maximum employment, and the Fed's new monetary policy framework which suggests the Fed should be more focused on inflation that has been above target for four years and is only going to deviate further from their 2.0% goal.

Hanging in the balance is the Fed's independence, which is constantly being chipped at through political pressure.

data.title: BDJ: This Fund Provides Diversification Benefits Along With A High Yield (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
The BlackRock Enhanced Equity Dividend Fund (NYSE:BDJ) is an interesting closed-end fund that investors who are in need of income can purchase as a method of achieving a very high level of income from their portfolios. This fund does very well at the provision of income, particularly for an equity fund. As of the time of writing, the fund boasts an 8.25% yield at the present share price. As we can see here, this is substantially higher than any of the major domestic equity indices:

Index/ETF

Current Yield

S&P 500 Index (SPY)

1.11%

NASDAQ-100 Index (QQQ)

0.49%

Russell 2000 Index (IWM)

1.06%

Dow Jones Industrial Average (DIA)

1.46%

This chart clearly illustrates one of the biggest problems facing income investors in today’s market. For the past several years, American equities have had incredibly low yields and fairly high valuations. As this chart shows, none of the broad-market index funds have yields higher than 2%, and even a 2% yield is very low compared to the 4% that can be obtained from any money market fund. Back in the 1990s, the federal funds rate was fairly consistently over 5%:

The average dividend yield of the S&P 500 Index was 4.03% throughout the 1970s and 1980s, and 1.90% throughout the 1990s:

We can even see that, as recently as the 2009 to 2019 period, the S&P 500 Index was consistently yielding 1.97% (almost 2%) on average. Basically, what happened is that as the Federal Reserve kept interest rates low over the period from 2009 through even today in order to lessen the burden of federal government borrowing and try to stimulate the economy in a high-leverage world, stock prices appreciated more rapidly than corporate dividends. This has made for a challenging environment for income investors due to the simple fact that an investor needs to have an enormous portfolio in order to generate sufficient income to pay their bills or support themselves in retirement. After all, given today’s S&P 500 dividend yield, a $1 million investment only generates $11,100 per year (less than $1,000 per month). Even with Social Security, that is not enough money to support a middle-class lifestyle.

Equities Seem Increasingly Necessary

At this point, some readers might point out that it is currently possible to achieve a higher level of income by investing in bonds or similar fixed-income securities. This is certainly true, as the major domestic bond indices do have much higher yields than common equities:

Index/ETF

TTM Yield

Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index (AGG)

3.83%

Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index (JNK)

6.54%

Preferred stocks also have higher yields than common equities, with the ICE Exchange-Listed Preferred & Hybrid Securities Index (PFF) having a trailing twelve-month yield of 6.43%. However, there is one major problem with these securities. They do not offer any sort of protection against inflation. A bond simply gives an investor back the same amount of nominal currency that they originally paid without regard for whether or not that nominal amount of currency still has the same purchasing power as it did when the bond was first issued.

There have been some analysts and economists in the financial media who have been promoting the idea that the inflationary pressures of the post-pandemic environment have been successfully beaten back, and the country is now in something that approaches a more “normal” level. This is, however, not the case. For starters, the July inflation report from the Bureau of Labor Statistics was nothing short of terrible. That report showed that inflation had consistently gotten worse from month to month over the April through June 2025 period. The report from earlier this month was equally as bad, as it showed a headline inflation rate of 2.7% and a core inflation rate of 3.10%. The Core Consumer Price Index’s year-over-year increase of 3.1% was higher than the 3.0% expected, and as Phoenix Capital Research explains, it appears that inflation is stabilizing at around 3%. That is well above the Federal Reserve’s 2.0% target.

Further evidence that inflation appears to be stabilizing at around 3% is that the percentage of items in the Core Consumer Price Index basket that have been experiencing annualized price increases of at least 3% is rising:

Furthermore, most components of the Consumer Price Index are now experiencing rising prices. This is a significant change from the disinflationary wave that occurred earlier this year, and it strengthens the case that inflation is here to stay, and it will not average 2% going forward.

This could be troubling for bonds, as should be immediately obvious from the above yields. If inflation is going to remain at 3%, rather than the Federal Reserve’s 2% target, then the 3.83% yield is not at all attractive. It is going to require most of that yield just to keep up with inflation, as a 3% inflation rate puts the real yield of the investment-grade bond index at less than 1%. This is hardly attractive for anyone who is trying to support themselves on the income that they earn from their investments. Junk bonds are not much better unless they are held in a tax-advantaged account, as a 6.54% yield is only 4.1202% for an investor in the top federal income tax bracket. That is barely over a 1% real yield if we assume a 3% inflation rate.

Despite all this, Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell said on Friday that he is open to the idea of cutting the federal funds target rate. Other members of the Federal Open Market Committee have made similar comments in recent months. Thus, it seems as though there is a high probability that the Federal Reserve will reduce interest rates before the end of the year. This results in two possible scenarios for bonds, neither of which are good for income investors:

  1. The Federal Reserve cuts short-term interest rates, and long-term bond yields rise. This is a similar scenario to what occurred at the end of 2024, when the Federal Reserve cut interest rates only to cause inflation to reaccelerate and bond investors to demand higher yields in an attempt to earn a positive real yield.
  2. Bond prices rise, yields fall, and investors in bonds end up losing purchasing power (negative real yields) over the lifetime of the bond.

The first scenario basically results in anyone buying bonds today suffering a capital loss as bond prices fall. The second option means that the purchasing power of an investor’s assets declines over time and ultimately makes it more difficult for their portfolio to support their lifestyle over the long term.

This leaves equities as the asset of choice in a situation where inflationary pressures remain strong, and the Federal Reserve is planning to lower interest rates anyway. After all, equities are much better than fixed-income securities at preserving the purchasing power of investors’ capital simply because rising prices should cause a company’s net income to rise, and equities are typically valued (rightly) based on the profits of a company. Unfortunately, equities in general have incredibly low yields, necessitating a huge portfolio in order to generate an income sufficient to support most lifestyles. Alternatively, an investor might consider a high-yielding closed-end equity fund such as the BlackRock Enhanced Equity Dividend Trust.

Peer Distribution Comparison

As already mentioned, the BlackRock Enhanced Equity Dividend Trust has a current yield of 8.25%. This yield is quite attractive when compared to the domestic equity indices, but it is not nearly as attractive when compared to some other closed-end funds that employ similar strategies. We can see that in this peer comparison:

The median yield of this group of covered call-writing closed-end equity funds is 8.25% of the BlackRock Enhanced Equity Dividend Fund. Thus, the fund’s yield is not extraordinary compared to other funds that employ a similar strategy. It is not especially disappointing, either, however, as it is still higher than half of the funds in the peer grouping.

One objective of many income-oriented investors is to maximize the amount of income that they earn from a given asset in their portfolios. As we can see from the chart above, the BlackRock Enhanced Equity Dividend Fund will not be the first fund that comes to mind to achieve this goal, given that it only has the median yield out of the group. However, the lack of an outsized yield does suggest that the market believes that this fund will be able to sustain its distribution going forward, and this is certainly something that is quite nice to see. After all, a sustainable income is also a goal shared by many investors for whom the generation of income is a priority, since most people in this group want something that deposits money into their brokerage accounts on a regular basis, and otherwise, they do not need to worry about it. A fund with a sustainable yield would generally be compatible with that goal.

The thesis that this fund’s distribution is sustainable is strengthened by the fact that the BlackRock Enhanced Equity Dividend Fund has grown its distribution over the past ten years. Back in August 2015, the BlackRock Enhanced Equity Dividend Fund was paying a distribution of $0.0467 per share monthly. Today, it pays a distribution of $0.0619 per share. This is a stark difference from the popular Eaton Vance option-income funds, many of which cut their distributions back in 2022. The BlackRock Enhanced Equity Dividend Fund has not cut its payout in more than ten years.

The fact that this fund has managed to increase its distribution over time has also been helpful for helping investors stay ahead of inflation and maintain their purchasing power. This fund has, overall, done remarkably well in this respect. The Consumer Price Index for All Urban Consumers is up 35.55% since August 2015. The distribution of the BlackRock Enhanced Equity Dividend Fund has risen by 32.55% over the same period. It is, therefore, not perfect, as its investors who spend the distributions that the fund pays out have seen their purchasing power decline very slightly over the period, but it has done much better than any fund that cut its distribution within the past decade.

Recent Performance Update

The last time that we discussed the BlackRock Enhanced Equity Dividend Fund was on March 4, 2024. Thus, almost eighteen months have passed since the last time that we looked at this fund. As we all know, a lot of things can occur in the capital markets over a period of eighteen months, and this most recent eighteen-month period was no exception to that. Let us take a few moments to review a few of the more significant events over the past year and a half.

The BlackRock Enhanced Equity Dividend Fund is somewhat different from many of the other covered call-writing funds available on the market. For example, the Eaton Vance funds that employ option strategies tend to have very high exposure to the information technology sector, which has been one of the drivers of the performance of both the S&P 500 Index (SP500) and the NASDAQ-100 Index over the past few years. The fact sheet for the Eaton Vance Enhanced Equity Income Fund provides this list of the largest positions in this fund:

As we can see here, the Eaton Vance Enhanced Equity Income Fund has seven technology companies – Microsoft (MSFT), Nvidia (NVDA), Apple (AAPL), Amazon.com (AMZN), Meta Platforms (META), Broadcom (AVGO), and Alphabet (GOOG) – among its ten largest positions. These companies alone account for 39.53% of that fund’s assets, which is obviously a great deal of exposure to the technology sector, let alone a great deal of exposure to only a handful of stocks.

The BlackRock Enhanced Equity Dividend Fund is different, though, as we can see from a simple look at the largest positions in the portfolio of this fund:

Amazon.com and Microsoft are the only two of the major technology companies in this fund’s largest positions list, and they only account for 5.11% of the fund’s portfolio. We also see that the BlackRock Enhanced Equity Dividend Fund has greater exposure to banks among its largest positions, as 10.32% of the holdings shown above are banks, than its peer fund, and this fund has more exposure to the financial sector throughout the remainder of its portfolio. The BlackRock Enhanced Equity Dividend Fund has a total of 24.29% of its assets invested in the financial sector and only a limited 11.46% invested in the technology sector:

The reason why this is important is that some of the major market events that occurred over the past eighteen months were pretty much limited to the technology sector. For example, the market selloff that was triggered by the release of DeepSeek’s (DEEPSEEK) chatbot was largely limited to the technology sector. The only reason why the S&P 500 Index also dropped due to that event is that the technology sector accounts for a substantial proportion of the entire S&P 500 Index.

This fund’s different asset allocation to the S&P 500 Index may have given the BlackRock Enhanced Equity Dividend Fund an advantage over many of the other covered call-writing equity closed-end funds in the market. As we can see here, the iShares U.S. Financials ETF (IYF), which tracks the performance of the common equities of American financial institutions, actually slightly outperformed the iShares U.S. Technology ETF (IYW), which tracks the American technology sector, over the past twelve months:

Furthermore, and perhaps more importantly, we can see that financials delivered their outperformance with far less volatility than technology. Starting from August 22, 2024, the technology sector bottomed out at 16.82% lower than its starting point, whereas financials were never down more than 2% from where they started. Thus, overall, the financials sector provided far more stability over the period than did the technology sector. Many income-seeking investors tend to be fairly risk-averse, so the fact that the BlackRock Enhanced Equity Dividend Fund appears to be investing in more stable assets than its peers could prove to be rather appealing. After all, this should, overall, allow the fund itself to exhibit lower volatility than some other equity funds. The BlackRock Enhanced Equity Dividend Trust also does not employ leverage, so this should further improve its stability.

We do, in fact, see a certain amount of stability in the fund’s share price since our last discussion:

This chart shows the share price performance of the BlackRock Enhanced Equity Dividend Fund compared to the S&P 500 Index since the March 4, 2024, publication date of my previous article on this fund. As we can see, the fund’s share price is up 11.11% compared to a 26.04% gain for the S&P 500 Index, so this fund obviously underperformed the index. This is not unexpected, however, since the fund’s strategy of writing covered call options against some of the stocks in its portfolio has the effect of reducing the potential upside that a fund can earn from the stocks in the portfolio. Basically, this fund is sacrificing some of its potential for capital gains in exchange for a higher level of income. It is up to the individual investor to determine whether or not this is an acceptable exchange.

If we also consider that the BlackRock Enhanced Equity Dividend Fund has a higher yield than the S&P 500 Index and incorporate that into our performance chart, we get this:

This chart shows the total return performance of the BlackRock Enhanced Equity Dividend Fund against the S&P 500 Total Return Index (SP500TR), assuming that investors opted to reinvest all dividends and distributions that were paid out over the period. This assumption makes the fund’s performance look very competitive with the index. It did underperform the index over the period, which is expected due to the covered call strategy, but the fund overall still did well enough that an income-oriented investor might not be completely turned off by it. Overall, the fund did deliver a 25.92% total return over a bit less than eighteen months, so it is still reasonably competitive with the long-term performance of the stock market while providing a much higher yield.

About The Fund

According to the fund’s website, the BlackRock Enhanced Equity Dividend Fund has the primary objective of providing its investors with a very high level of current income and current gains. This objective makes a certain amount of sense, given two elements of the fund’s portfolio:

  1. The BlackRock Enhanced Equity Dividend Fund invests primarily in dividend-paying common stocks. These dividends serve as a source of income for the fund, which it can then distribute to its shareholders.
  2. The fund writes covered call options against some of the stocks in its portfolio. It receives an upfront premium from whoever purchases this option, which serves as a synthetic dividend from the stock against which the option is written and is in addition to the actual dividend that the common stock pays. As I showed in a previous article, these synthetic dividends can effectively convert almost any stock into a very high-yielding common equity security.

Let us address point one, which is the fact that the BlackRock Enhanced Equity Dividend Fund invests its assets primarily in dividend-paying common stocks. It is unlikely that this is actually going to be a surprise to anyone who is familiar with most of the companies on this fund’s top ten holdings list. After all, most of them currently have fairly high dividend yields. We can see that quite clearly on this chart:

The only company that is included in the fund’s ten largest positions that does not pay a dividend is Amazon.com. Furthermore, there are only two companies – First Citizens Bancshares and Microsoft Corporation – that possess yields that are lower than the 1.11% yield of the broader S&P 500 Index. Thus, we can clearly see that the BlackRock Enhanced Equity Dividend Fund is indeed focusing its efforts on investing in dividend-paying stocks. Unlike some other funds that are in the market, this one is not claiming to invest in dividend stocks and then assigning a high weighting to low-growth technology stocks.

There have been a substantial number of changes to this fund’s portfolio since the last time that we discussed it. In particular, American International Group (AIG), Shell (SHEL), Kraft Heinz (KHC), Leidos Holdings (LDOS), General Motors (GM), L3Harris (LHX), and Fidelity National Information Services (FIS) have all been removed from the fund’s portfolio. The new additions that we see over the past eighteen months are SS&C Technologies, Amazon.com, Microsoft, CVS Health, Becton, Dickinson & Company, Medtronic, and BP. The fact that seven of the fund’s largest ten positions have been changed over the period might lead us to believe that the BlackRock Enhanced Equity Dividend Fund engages in a great deal of trading activity. However, the fund’s most recent annual report states that this fund only had a 40% turnover rate for the twelve-month period that ended on December 31, 2024. This is actually slightly higher than what this fund has averaged in previous years:

(all figures are for the twelve-month period that ended on December 31 of the respective year)

As we can see, this fund has historically had slightly lower turnover ratios in three of the past five years. As such, it does not appear to be engaging in as much trading activity as we might think from the sheer number of changes to the largest positions list. It is possible that some of the changes were actually caused by one stock outperforming another in the marketplace and thus changing their relative weightings.

Distribution Analysis

The primary objective of the BlackRock Enhanced Equity Dividend Fund is to provide its investors with a very high level of current income and current gains. In accordance with this objective, the fund pays a regular monthly distribution of $0.0619 per share ($0.7428 per share annually) to its investors. This gives the fund an 8.25% distribution yield at the current share price, which is reasonable but by no means exceptional among its peers. It is quite attractive in the current market, though, and this is one of the few equity funds that has not cut its distribution for more than ten years.

Naturally, though, we want to make sure that this fund can afford the distribution that it pays out. After all, the last thing that we want is to purchase a fund as a source of income and then have it suddenly cut the distribution. For this purpose, we will consult the fund’s annual report for the twelve-month period that ended on December 31, 2024. This report is, admittedly, several months old at this point, but it is the most recent financial report that the fund has released as of the time of writing. It is also a more recent report than the one that was available to us the last time that we discussed this fund, so it should work pretty well for the provision of an update.

For the twelve-month period that ended on December 31, 2024, the BlackRock Enhanced Equity Dividend Fund reported a net investment income of $31,964,398. This was not sufficient to cover the $125,237,041 that the fund paid out in distributions over the same period.

Fortunately, the fund was able to make up for the difference through capital gains. For the twelve-month period that ended on December 31, 2024, the BlackRock Enhanced Equity Dividend Fund reported net realized gains of $71,556,715 along with $61,248,610 in net unrealized gains. The sum of the fund’s net investment income, net realized gains, and net unrealized gains was $164,769,723, which was sufficient to cover the distributions and still leave the fund some investment profits left over. Thus, it technically did cover its distributions over the full-year period.

However, the fund’s net investment income plus net realized gains were only $103,521,113 over the full-year period. That was not sufficient to fully cover the $125,237,041 that was paid out in distributions. The fund only managed full coverage due to its net unrealized gains, which could be problematic as a market correction can erase these gains. For now, though, it appears that the fund is fine due to the fact that its net asset value is up 5.60% year-to-date:

Thus, not only has this fund not surrendered the unrealized gains that it earned last year, but it has also completely covered all of the distributions that it has paid out year-to-date. Thus, for the time being, the distribution of the BlackRock Enhanced Equity Dividend Fund appears to be sustainable, and there is nothing that we need to worry about.

Valuation

Shares of the BlackRock Enhanced Equity Dividend Fund are currently trading at a 4.96% discount on net asset value. This is slightly worse than the 5.46% discount that this fund’s shares have traded at on average over the past month.

Historically, the BlackRock Enhanced Equity Dividend Fund has traded at a better price than it has today. Over the past three years, the shares of this fund have traded at prices ranging from a 15.30% discount to a 3.09% premium on net asset value. The average price over the period has been a 6.59% discount, which is a larger discount than the fund currently possesses.

Here is how the current price of the BlackRock Enhanced Equity Dividend Fund compares with its peers:

The median price of this peer group is the 4.65% discount of the First Trust Enhanced Equity Income Fund. The BlackRock Enhanced Equity Dividend Fund has a slightly larger discount than that, so this price is certainly not overpriced when compared to its peers. While it is currently slightly more expensive than its three-year average, it is not near the top of its three-year range, so it is probably not too badly priced right now.

Expenses

The website for the BlackRock Enhanced Equity Dividend Fund states that the fund has a 0.88% expense ratio:

BlackRock funds in general tend to be very reasonably priced, and this one is no exception to that.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the BlackRock Enhanced Equity Dividend Fund is a closed-end fund that employs an equity covered call-writing strategy. This strategy gives the fund a fairly high yield relative to many other equity funds, but one of the better aspects of this fund is that it has a very different portfolio than many other domestic equity funds. In particular, this fund does not have a very high allocation to the technology sector. This could allow it to work pretty well as a diversifier for a portfolio of other funds that do have technology exposure. After all, there is always a risk to having too much exposure to one single industry sector, and thus diversification is necessary. This fund could provide diversification along with a very high yield.
